turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Alito has our opinion in case 15-927, SCA Hygiene Products versus First Quality Baby Products.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: This case concerns a question that I am sure is on everyone's mind and that is the interaction between the equitable defense of laches in the statue of limitations, although maybe it is of interest to the patent lawyers who are here for the second case this morning. In any event, this subject is one that we explored three years ago in Petrella versus Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer. Laches is a defense that prevents unreasonable prejudicial delay on the part of a plaintiff brining suit. It is applied by judges on a case-by-case basis taking into account the facts of a particular case. On the other hand there is a legislative enactment that imposes a more or less hard and fast rule. So, can they both be applied in the same case? In Petrella, to which I referred, we held that laches cannot preclude a claim for damages incurred within the Copyright Act’s three-year limitations period. Our holding was based on both separation of powers principles and the role of laches in equity. As to the separation of powers, we reasoned that Congress in enacting the statute of limitations makes a judgment about how long it is willing to allow a plaintiff to wait before filing suit. Therefore, it would usurp Congress’ judgment if the judiciary were to permit a defendant to cut short the limitations period by raising a laches defense within that period. As to equity, we noted that laches developed in equity courts to play a gap-filling role where there was no statute of limitations. But where there is statute of limitations there was no gap to fill and laches has no role to play. Petrella concerned Copyright; the present case concerns the Patent Act’s six-year statute of limitation on damages. Under that statute, a plaintiff can recover damages for patent infringements that occurred within six years before the suit was filed. We see no relevant distinction between this statute of limitations and the one in Petrella. Therefore, the principles in Petrella govern this case. The federal circuit reached the opposite conclusion. The federal circuit thought that case law at the time of the passage of the Patent Act in 1952 made it clear that laches can be asserted to bar damages claims within the Patent Act’s six-year limitations period and that the Patent Act was meant to incorporate that rule. But we disagree with this evaluation of the pre-1952 case law. There was one big aspect of that pre-1952 case law to which the Federal Circuit did not give sufficient attention and that was the fact that this Court had addressed the question in a number of cases albeit not in the Patent Act context. We see no contrary consensus in the lower courts’ Patent Act cases that is sufficient to overcome the statements in our own decisions. So for these and other reasons set out on our opinion, we conclude that laches cannot be interposed as a defense against damages claims within the Patent Act’s six-year limitations period. The judgment of federal circuit is vacated in part and the case is remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Breyer has filed a dissenting opinion.
